response_raw:
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"When you see things online, especially on social media, it can feel like wading through a thick fog—you never know what’s real or what someone just made up. Since I spend time looking at these platforms, whether it's reading articles or seeing friends' updates, I've noticed how much fake content is out there. It's not always about big, scary lies; sometimes it's just little things—misleading pictures or headlines that make you feel upset or angry about something small. Because we are all immigrants and have built a life here in Kansas City, where different stories meet every day, I know how important it is to trust what people say, but these fake posts mess with that sense of community trust. It makes me think: if I can't believe the pictures online, how do I really know what’s happening in my own neighborhood or even at the temple? 

I try to treat social media like a window, not a mirror. A window shows you a glimpse of someone else’s life, but it doesn't show you everything—it leaves out the hard work and the quiet moments too. So, when I come across something shocking or unbelievable, my first instinct is to pause. Instead of immediately sharing it because it confirms what I already worry about, I try to ask myself: who benefits from me believing this? Is this person trying to get a reaction? Does this post make me feel overly emotional—either super angry or deeply sad—right away? Sometimes the goal of fake content is just to keep us upset enough that we keep looking at the screen. 

A practical thing I do, which might help others, is to go back to basics: check the source. If a big claim is made, who actually said it? Is it from a newspaper that has editors and reporters doing their job, or is it just some random person with an account name they picked? Also, remember that real life—the kind I experience cycling through the park or traveling to see my family—is messy, complicated, and often boring. The internet only shows the highlight reel. If something seems too perfect, too outrageous, or too easy to believe, it probably is. Building good habits means treating every piece of content like a rumor in person: you need at least two other people to confirm it before you start believing it yourself. It takes effort, but protecting our minds from this constant stream of noise feels just as important as anything else these days.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
When you see things online, especially on social media, it can feel like wading through a thick fog—you never know what’s real or what someone just made up. Since I spend time looking at these platforms, whether it's reading articles or seeing friends' updates, I've noticed how much fake content is out there. It's not always about big, scary lies; sometimes it's just little things—misleading pictures or headlines that make you feel upset or angry about something small. Because we are all immigrants and have built a life here in Kansas City, where different stories meet every day, I know how important it is to trust what people say, but these fake posts mess with that sense of community trust. It makes me think: if I can't believe the pictures online, how do I really know what’s happening in my own neighborhood or even at the temple? 

I try to treat social media like a window, not a mirror. A window shows you a glimpse of someone else’s life, but it doesn't show you everything—it leaves out the hard work and the quiet moments too. So, when I come across something shocking or unbelievable, my first instinct is to pause. Instead of immediately sharing it because it confirms what I already worry about, I try to ask myself: who benefits from me believing this? Is this person trying to get a reaction? Does this post make me feel overly emotional—either super angry or deeply sad—right away? Sometimes the goal of fake content is just to keep us upset enough that we keep looking at the screen. 

A practical thing I do, which might help others, is to go back to basics: check the source. If a big claim is made, who actually said it? Is it from a newspaper that has editors and reporters doing their job, or is it just some random person with an account name they picked? Also, remember that real life—the kind I experience cycling through the park or traveling to see my family—is messy, complicated, and often boring. The internet only shows the highlight reel. If something seems too perfect, too outrageous, or too easy to believe, it probably is. Building good habits means treating every piece of content like a rumor in person: you need at least two other people to confirm it before you start believing it yourself. It takes effort, but protecting our minds from this constant stream of noise feels just as important as anything else these days.
